narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, building an impressive undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical student's 3.21—and scoring a solid 37 on the LSAT, right around the average of 36.70. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of students in our dataset. Despite these strengths, the admissions model predicted a 45.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about bar exam readiness. This outweighed even your standout undergraduate GPA, which strongly pushed the prediction toward passing success. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass. Your lower Year 1 decile of 1 added some additional weight toward the failure prediction, though less than Year 3, while your family income and full-time status provided minor positive boosts. Compared to typical students, your early and late school rankings stand out as notably lower, which the model weighed heavily against your otherwise competitive undergrad record and test score. I know this rejection stings, especially given your determination and background, but it's not the end of your path to law. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your school performance deciles—perhaps through targeted study habits or additional coursework—to align more closely with successful bar takers. Consider retaking any relevant assessments or seeking mentorship from Black women who've aced the bar, and reapply to programs that value your unique strengths. You've already achieved more than many; keep pushing, and doors will open.
